exercises.title: Goodhart's law and why benchmarks saturate
exercises.prompt: Explain Goodhart's law in the context of LLM benchmarks, why standard benchmarks (MMLU, HumanEval) saturate and stop differentiating frontier models, and why 'the only eval that matters is YOUR eval'.
exercises.hint: When a measure becomes a target, it stops measuring well. Labs optimize for / contaminate benchmarks → scores rise without real capability. Saturation: top models all near ceiling, no signal. Your task/data is what actually matters and isn't gamed.
exercises.solution:
**Goodhart's law:** *'when a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure.'* Once a benchmark becomes the thing labs compete on, they **optimize for it** — directly (training on similar data, format-tuning) and indirectly (**contamination**: benchmark data leaks into training, Lesson 3). So **scores rise without a matching rise in true capability**; the number stops reflecting what it was meant to measure.

**Why MMLU/HumanEval saturate:** 
- **Ceiling effects:** frontier models all score in the high 80s-90s on MMLU, so the benchmark **can no longer differentiate** them — everyone's near the top, and the remaining gap is noise/contamination, not capability.
- **Contamination:** these public benchmarks are old and all over the web, so they've almost certainly leaked into training sets — inflating scores.
- **Narrowness:** a multiple-choice knowledge test (MMLU) or function-completion test (HumanEval) measures a **slice** of capability; a model can ace them while failing trivially elsewhere — the lesson's example: MMLU goes up while models **still can't reliably count the R's in 'strawberry'** (a tokenization-level failure, Lesson 1). High benchmark ≠ good at *your* task.

**Why 'the only eval that matters is YOUR eval':** public leaderboards are gamed, saturated, contaminated, and measure generic slices. **Your specific use case** — your prompts, your data, your success criteria, your edge cases — is:
- **Not in anyone's training set** (so not contaminated/gamed),
- **Actually predictive** of how the model performs *for you*,
- **Discriminating** (it surfaces the differences between models that matter for your task, where saturated benchmarks show none).
So you build a **custom eval on your task with your data** (next exercises) and treat public benchmarks as, at best, a rough prior. The practical rule: **trust the eval you built and decontaminated for your problem, not the leaderboard.**

exercises.title: Building an eval harness with the right metrics
exercises.prompt: Implement an evaluation harness that handles multiple-choice (exact match) and open-ended tasks, and explain when to use exact match, F1, BLEU/ROUGE, and LLM-as-judge. What are the pitfalls of each metric?
exercises.hint: MC → exact match / accuracy. Short extractive → F1/exact match. Generation → BLEU/ROUGE (overlap, weak for semantics). Open-ended quality → LLM-as-judge (scalable but biased). Pitfalls: parsing, surface-form mismatch, n-gram blindness to meaning, judge biases.
exercises.solution:
```python
import numpy as np

def exact_match(pred, gold):
    return normalize(pred) == normalize(gold)        # case/punct/space-normalized

def token_f1(pred, gold):
    p, g = set(normalize(pred).split()), set(normalize(gold).split())
    if not p or not g: return 0.0
    overlap = len(p & g)
    prec, rec = overlap/len(p), overlap/len(g)
    return 0.0 if prec+rec == 0 else 2*prec*rec/(prec+rec)

def run_eval(model, examples, kind='mc'):
    scores = []
    for ex in examples:
        out = model(ex['prompt'])
        if kind == 'mc':
            scores.append(exact_match(extract_choice(out), ex['answer']))
        elif kind == 'qa':
            scores.append(token_f1(out, ex['answer']))
        # kind == 'open': hand to an LLM judge (see below)
    return np.mean(scores)
```

**When to use which metric (and its pitfall):**
- **Exact match / accuracy** — **multiple-choice and tasks with one canonical answer**. Pitfall: **parsing/surface-form fragility** — the model says 'The answer is C' or 'four' instead of 'C'/'4', and naive matching marks it wrong. You need robust answer extraction and normalization, and constrained output formats.
- **Token F1** — **short extractive answers (QA)** where partial overlap counts. Pitfall: rewards **word overlap, not correctness** — a fluent wrong answer sharing words scores partially; synonyms/paraphrases score low despite being right.
- **BLEU / ROUGE** — **translation/summarization** (n-gram overlap with references). Pitfall: **blind to meaning** — they reward surface n-gram overlap, penalize valid paraphrases, reward fluent nonsense that copies reference words, and correlate weakly with human judgment for open-ended generation. Use only where a reference is meaningful and overlap is a reasonable proxy.
- **LLM-as-judge** — **open-ended quality** (helpfulness, coherence, style) where no reference exists. Scalable and flexible. Pitfalls: **judge biases** — position bias (favors first/second), **verbosity/length bias** (prefers longer answers), **self-preference** (favors its own model family), stylistic bias (slick over correct), and it can be **wrong/inconsistent**. Must be **calibrated against human labels**, run with **randomized/swapped positions**, and validated — it's a fast proxy, not ground truth (same 'calibrate the measurer' principle as Phase 6/8 evals).
**General pitfalls:** automatic metrics measure proxies; pick the metric that matches the task's *real* objective, combine several, control for format/parsing, and anchor on **human spot-checks** for anything open-ended.

exercises.title: Designing a trustworthy custom eval suite
exercises.prompt: Design a custom evaluation suite for a real product use case. Cover: building a representative + adversarial dataset, decontamination, choosing metrics, controlling variance/statistical significance, avoiding overfitting to your own eval, and the role of human evaluation and continuous (production) evals. Why is eval design itself a competitive advantage in 2026?
exercises.hint: Sample real user data + hard/edge cases + safety cases; hold it private (decontaminate). Pick metrics per task. Enough examples + CIs for significance. Don't train on the eval / rotate it. Human eval as anchor; log production + run live evals. Good evals = you can tell real improvements apart = ship faster/safer.
exercises.solution:
**1. Build a representative + adversarial dataset.** Sample **real prompts from your actual use case** (or realistic synthetic ones) covering the **distribution your users send**, weighted by frequency and importance. Then deliberately add **hard cases**: edge cases, ambiguous inputs, long inputs, multilingual/code if relevant, known failure modes, and **adversarial/safety cases** (jailbreaks, prompt injection, harmful requests, PII). A good suite tests both the **common path** (does it work for typical use?) and the **tails** (where models actually differ and break).

**2. Decontaminate and keep it private.** Make sure your eval data is **not in any model's training set** — use **fresh/private data**, add **canary strings**, and keep the gold answers **out of public repos**. A leaked eval is a contaminated eval (Lesson 3); a **private, held-out** suite is the only one you can trust over time, and it's why your eval beats the leaderboard.

**3. Choose metrics per task type** (previous exercise): exact match for structured outputs, F1 for extractive QA, **LLM-as-judge (calibrated) or human eval** for open-ended quality, plus **task-specific checks** (does the code run? is the JSON valid? is the cited fact real?). Define **clear pass/fail criteria** tied to *your* success definition, not a generic score.

**4. Control variance and significance.** LLM outputs are stochastic and metrics are noisy, so:
- Use **enough examples** that differences are meaningful, and report **confidence intervals** (bootstrap).
- **Fix decoding settings** (temperature, seeds) or average over multiple samples.
- When comparing model A vs B, require the **difference to exceed the noise** (CI excludes zero) before calling it a real improvement — many 'wins' are within noise.

**5. Avoid overfitting to your own eval.** Your private eval can *itself* become a Goodhart target if you tune against it repeatedly. Defenses: keep a **separate held-out slice** you rarely look at, **rotate/refresh** eval data periodically, and never train directly on eval examples. The eval must stay an **honest, out-of-sample** signal.

**6. Human evaluation as the anchor.** For open-ended quality, **human (or carefully-calibrated LLM-judge) preference comparisons** are the ground truth that automatic metrics are validated against (Phase 8.14, 6.17). Use automatic metrics for **fast iteration**, periodic **human eval** for trustworthy ship/no-ship calls, and **blind, randomized A/B** with significance for model comparisons.

**7. Continuous / production evals.** Evaluation isn't one-time: **log real production inputs/outputs**, run **live evals** on sampled traffic, track quality/regressions over time, and feed hard production failures back into the eval set. Models, prompts, and usage drift; your eval must be a **living system**, not a static report.

**Why eval design is a competitive advantage in 2026:** when benchmarks are saturated, gamed, and contaminated, **the team with the best private eval can actually tell whether a change is an improvement.** That means you can:
- **Pick the right model/prompt/fine-tune** for *your* task instead of chasing leaderboard noise,
- **Catch regressions** before users do,
- **Iterate faster** (a trustworthy signal makes every experiment decisive) and **ship safer** (you measure the safety/edge cases that matter to you).
Everyone has access to the same models; **few have a rigorous, decontaminated, task-specific eval.** That measurement capability — knowing *what's actually better for your problem* — is the moat. As the lesson says, the only eval that matters is **yours**, and building it well is one of the highest-leverage skills in applied LLM engineering.
